original_abstract:
Digital twin (DT) technology has garnered significant attention across scientific and engineering disciplines due to its transformative potential. However, the rapid exploration of this technology has led to fragmented methodologies tailored to specific sectors like manufacturing and automation. The civil engineering industry, in particular, has struggled with this lack of cohesion, often relying on external frameworks that prioritize the operations and maintenance phases. While Building Information Modeling (BIM) is widely used during planning and design, the transient and dynamic nature of the construction phase remains a major hurdle for seamless DT integration. 

In this article, we propose a phase-based framework for developing digital twins within the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) industry. We begin by clarifying DT as both a concept and a service, establishing a standardized five-level scale system for its implementation. We then provide a systematic literature review of conventional techniques utilized at each stage of a project’s lifecycle, identifying critical enabling technologies such as computer vision for enhanced sensing and the Internet of Things (IoT) for robust data integration. Ultimately, this work seeks to reframe the digital twin as a vital tool for the entire lifecycle of civil engineering projects, encouraging researchers to adopt a more holistic perspective toward digital integration.
humanized_abstract:
Although Digital twin (DT) technology is becoming a trendy topic in the science and engineering communities, the current progress is carried out in an ad-hoc fashion, with little unity in the approaches and applications at the different levels (i.e., production engineering, automation, or quality control) and for the different applications and sectors considered (for instance, manufacturing or civil engineering). To illustrate this, in civil engineering applications and processes, current approaches have to rely heavily on practices and techniques that are not originally related to the operational and maintenance phases of the structures and infrastructure objects. For example, Building Information Modeling (BIM) is mainly a planning and design tool and the short-term, dynamic, and transient nature of the building and assembling processes, constitutes an important, but currently unresolved challenge for the implementation of DT technology within the civil structure engineering practice.

This study puts forward a phase-based approach to create digital twins in the context of the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) sector. Firstly, the concept and service of DT is introduced and classified using a five-level scale system. Secondly, a systematic literature review on the common methods, technologies, tools, and approaches used at each stage of the construction project lifecycle is carried out. A range of techniques, such as computer vision for real time sensing and Internet of Things (IoT) for real time data integration are identified as key enabling technologies. This research aims to redefine the role of DT along the entire lifecycle of civil engineering infrastructure projects and therefore calls for a more integrated approach to digitalization within this context.
